cards.text: An {{c1::lvalue}} is an expression that evaluates to an {{c2::identifiable object or function}} (has an identity).
An {{c1::rvalue}} is an expression that is {{c2::not}} an lvalue. Rvalues are typically {{c3::temporary}} or {{c4::literal}} values.

cards.question: Which expressions are lvalues?
cards.answer: Expressions 1 and 4 are lvalues. Expression 1 (x) identifies a variable, and expression 4 (x = 6) evaluates to x.
cards.explanation: Variables and assignment expressions are lvalues. Literals (5) and temporary results (x + 1) are rvalues.

cards.front: Can you take the address of an lvalue?
cards.back: Yes. An lvalue has an identifiable memory location, so you can take its address using the address-of operator (&).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot take the address of rvalues. Both 5 and (x + 1) are rvalues with no identifiable memory location.
cards.explanation: The address-of operator requires an lvalue because only lvalues have persistent memory addresses.

cards.front: Why must the left operand of an assignment be an lvalue?
cards.back: Because assignment requires modifying an identifiable object in memory. An lvalue has an identity and memory location, while an rvalue is temporary.

cards.concept: Quick test to determine if an expression is an lvalue
cards.explanation:
Ask: Can I take the address of this expression?
  • If YES: it's an lvalue (has an identity)
  • If NO: it's an rvalue (temporary or literal)

cards.text: Variables are {{c1::lvalues}} because they have an {{c2::identifiable memory location}}, while literals like 5 are {{c3::rvalues}}.
